results.possibleIdentity: Martin Aaron was a professional materials consultant and engineer active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is historically recognized for his involvement with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization founded in 1966 by Billy Klüver, Robert Rauschenberg, Robert Whitman, and Fred Waldhauer to facilitate collaborations between the artistic and technical communities. As part of E.A.T.’s Technical Services program, Aaron provided specialized expertise to artists, helping them utilize advanced industrial materials and processes in their creative works. His technical competencies included composites, plastics, glass, reinforced sub-strata combinations, adhesives, welding, electronics, and mechanics. He operated professionally out of New York City and Wingdale, New York. Beyond his consulting work, he was associated with Martin Aaron, Inc., a company founded in the late 1960s (specifically 1969) in Camden, New Jersey, which focused on drum reconditioning and recycling—an industry that utilized his extensive knowledge of materials, welding, and chemical handling. This business location later became a notable Superfund site after its closure in the late 1990s.
